---
title: "Mathematical Foundations of Operational Subjective Probability on Misesian Microfoundations"
subtitle: "Brief for Erin Beckman & Emmy Heywood"
author: "TJB"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrartcl
    number-sections: false
    fontsize: 11pt
    fontfamily: mathpazo
    geometry:
      - top=1in
      - bottom=1in
      - left=1in
      - right=1in
  gfm: default
---

*Re: Bayesians in the Catallaxy: From Mises--Hayek to de Finetti*

## Framing

The paper argues that de Finetti's operational subjective probability theory and Austrian catallactics share a single foundation --- and that, properly understood, de Finetti is doing for probability what Mises was doing for praxeology, on the same Mengerian theory of price. Most probabilists meet de Finetti only as the author of the representation theorem, treated as a curious result inside an otherwise Kolmogorov-formalized subject. We need something more: a mathematically rigorous account of *what de Finetti's system looks like when its economic foundations are taken seriously as foundations*, and what new mathematical questions that opens.

Mises and Hayek themselves never formalized their system. The math has to be built --- carefully, and correctly, because the paper's whole claim depends on it.

## Collaboration structure

Erin is principal on the mathematical content --- the theorem statements, the formal structure, the probability-and-particle-systems machinery. Emmy joins as collaborator, bringing applied-economics training that the math side of the team otherwise lacks. The natural division of labor:

- **Erin** drives the mathematical work: the formal statements, the proofs (or careful identification of what is proved and what remains conjectural), and the mapping from de Finettian and Misesian primitives onto rigorous probabilistic structure.
- **Emmy** is the natural interlocutor between Erin's probability/PDE world and the catallactic literature she will be encountering for the first time. Concretely: reading and summarizing the economic sources that motivate the math (Mises, Hayek, de Finetti, McCall on exchangeable search, Lewin--Cachanosky on Austrian capital theory), drafting expository prose that translates between the disciplines, and supporting the literature legwork in question E below. Emmy's economics training is the bridge that keeps the mathematical work anchored in the project's economic claims rather than drifting into formalism for its own sake.

TJB will be the principal economic interlocutor for both of you, available for the catallactic-economics questions that arise as you work through the readings.

## The mathematical questions

### A. Formalizing the bridge from Misesian appraisement to de Finettian prevision

- De Finetti's primitive is the prevision $\Pr(\cdot)$ --- simultaneously price, probability, and prevision (foresight). Mises's primitive is the entrepreneurial appraisement of value in money prices. The paper claims these are the same primitive, viewed through different lenses. *How do we make this precise.* What is the formal structure?
- What is the smallest set of axioms --- economically interpretable, not borrowed wholesale from Kolmogorov --- from which the standard probability calculus is recovered as a theorem of coherence? De Finetti gives an outline; we need a clean modern statement that an Austrian could read and a probabilist could verify.

### B. Exchangeability as the formal expression of the Hayekian epistemic condition

- The paper claims exchangeability is "the probabilistic formalization of the Hayekian epistemic condition" --- that an agent reasoning under genuine dispersed-knowledge uncertainty is exactly an agent whose subjective beliefs are exchangeable. State and prove (or at minimum carefully formulate) this as a theorem. What is the right statement? What are the necessary and sufficient conditions?
- What is the precise relationship between de Finetti's representation theorem in this setting and Hayek's "use of knowledge in society"? The representation theorem says exchangeable sequences are mixtures over i.i.d. distributions --- which looks suspiciously like the move from a single agent's coherent belief to an emergent objective-looking pattern. Is this the formal structure of a Hayekian spontaneous order at the level of beliefs?

### C. Endogenous stochasticity and interacting subjective Bayesians *(natural anchor for the particle-systems perspective)*

- The methodology section claims that stochasticity in market processes is *endogenous*: generated by the interaction of many agents with subjective beliefs, not drawn from an external distribution. This is, mathematically, a statement about an interacting particle system in which the "particles" are coherent subjective Bayesians whose beliefs update on observed prices and trades.
- What does the right mathematical object look like? The natural questions: existence and uniqueness of such systems; macroscopic limit theorems; conditions under which an emergent "objective-looking" price process arises from purely subjective agents; conditions under which it does *not* (volatility, regime change, market microstructure phenomena). The particle-systems toolkit is the natural one for these questions.
- The "communism of models" critique can be sharpened mathematically: rational-expectations equilibrium is a pathological special case in which the particle system has been collapsed to a single particle. Can we articulate this rigorously.

### D. Exchangeable search and the option/search isomorphism

- McCall's exchangeable search theory is taken in the paper as the bridge between de Finetti and Austrian entrepreneurship. We need a clean modern statement of exchangeable search and its key results, suitable for a JEP audience but verifiable by specialists.
- The paper relies on the mathematical equivalence between sequential search and the perpetual American call option. State this carefully: what hypotheses are needed, and where does the equivalence break down? By building upon Lewin & Cachanosky we argue Austrian capital theory becomes exchangeable real-options theory --- what is the precise mathematical content of that claim?

### E. What needs *new* math vs. what can be assembled from existing literature

- For each of A--D: separate (i) results that exist in the literature and need only to be cited and (lightly) restated, from (ii) results that are folkloric and need a clean formal statement, from (iii) genuinely new theorems that the paper's argument requires us to prove. The honest accounting matters --- the paper is a JEP submission, not the place to develop deep new mathematics, but we should know which of our claims rest on results that nobody has actually proved. We develop the argument further in subsequent work.

## What we are not asking

- Re-examine whether the de Finetti / Mises--Hayek synthesis is the right framing. That is the paper's economic argument; the math is in service of it.
- Treat probability as Kolmogorov's measure-theoretic edifice and translate de Finetti into it. The point is precisely that the *operational* foundation is prior to the measure-theoretic one. (This is the place where the subjectivist re-orientation is going to be most uncomfortable; it's worth flagging gently.)

## Suggested deliverable

A jointly authored working note (8--15 pages) organized around A--E, with explicit theorem/proof structure where appropriate, and a clean accounting under E of what is settled vs. what we still owe the reader. This will become the mathematical backbone of section 2 (de Finetti pillar) and section 3 (the triangular arbitrage insights), but we are not asking you to draft those sections. A suggested workflow: Emmy summarizes the economic readings in working memos that Erin uses as input; Erin formulates and drafts the mathematical content; both of you iterate the joint write-up.
